name: assassin boots
shortDescription: Assassin boots are dark, soft-soled footwear made for rooftops, back stairs, and other places where a loud step can become a confession. The leather is flexible but reinforced at the heel, suggesting owners who expect to run as often as they stalk.
flavorText: “The best assassin boots are noticed only by the floorboards that failed to complain.”

name: black boots
shortDescription: A practical pair of black boots, plain enough for patrols and respectable enough for a guarded doorway. Their dark finish hides road grime well, which is one reason soldiers, porters, and secretive travelers all keep buying them.
flavorText: “Black boots match mud, mourning, and bad intentions equally well.”

name: black shoes
shortDescription: Black shoes with a modest polish, fit for clerks, stewards, junior courtiers, and merchants who spend more time indoors than on roads. They are not heroic footwear, but they know their way around ledgers, thresholds, and careful first impressions.
flavorText: “A polished black shoe can open doors a muddy boot must kick.”

name: rough boots
shortDescription: Rough boots stitched for use rather than admiration, with thick leather, hard soles, and little patience for decoration. Farmers, haulers, guards, and unlucky travelers all trust them because the road does not care how handsome footwear is.
flavorText: “Rough boots are ugly in town and beautiful in rain.”

name: rough shoes
shortDescription: Rough shoes made from plain leather and practical stitching, cheap enough to replace and sturdy enough to deserve repair. They belong to workshop floors, kitchen yards, and anyone whose work begins before fashion wakes up.
flavorText: “A rough shoe asks only for a foot and a reason to keep moving.”

name: fancy laced boots
shortDescription: Fancy laced boots with polished uppers, neat cords, and enough ornament to make walking look almost ceremonial. They are bought by officers, performers, and young nobles who have not yet learned that mud ignores status.
flavorText: “Fine laces say the wearer had time, servants, or both.”

name: fancy laced shoes
shortDescription: Fancy laced shoes designed for halls, banquets, and public conversations where ankles are apparently part of diplomacy. Their stitching is tidy, their leather is smooth, and their usefulness ends roughly where cobblestones become honest.
flavorText: “A fancy shoe survives longest on floors that have already been swept.”

name: strong boots
shortDescription: Strong boots built with heavy soles, firm stitching, and the sort of leather that expects trouble. They are dependable trade stock for guards, miners, caravan hands, and anyone who measures footwear by how long it resists apology.
flavorText: “Strong boots do not make a road shorter, but they make it less personal.”

name: winter boots
shortDescription: Winter boots lined against cold and shaped to keep slush, snow, and bad judgment outside. They are bulky in a warm market but priceless once the wind begins treating toes as negotiable goods.
flavorText: “In Frosthaven, warm boots are not comfort. They are policy.”

name: work boots
shortDescription: Work boots with scuffed toes, reinforced seams, and no illusions about clean floors. They are made for warehouses, docks, yards, and all the places where honest labor steps on nails before breakfast.
flavorText: “A work boot earns its shape by being mistreated correctly.”

name: vest
shortDescription: A plain vest that adds pockets, warmth, and a little respectability without committing to a full coat. It is common among clerks, traders, tavern keepers, and men who believe every useful garment should hold at least three small debts.
flavorText: “A vest is where a merchant hides pencils, coins, and patience.”

name: chain boots
shortDescription: Chain boots made from interlinked mail over a sturdy underlayer, offering protection where blades and bites often arrive low. They are noisy, heavy, and reassuring, which is exactly the compromise armored people expect.
flavorText: “Chain boots make every step sound prepared.”

name: leather boots
shortDescription: Leather boots of the ordinary reliable kind, suited to roads, shops, stables, and modest travel. A good pair softens with use and tells a careful buyer more about its owner than most introductions do.
flavorText: “Leather remembers the foot, the road, and the rain.”

name: plate boots
shortDescription: Plate boots are armored footwear for people who expect the ground battle to include axes, claws, and other rude interruptions. They protect well but walk loudly, trading stealth for the satisfying confidence of not losing toes.
flavorText: “Plate boots are diplomacy for the lower body.”

name: tall boots
shortDescription: Tall boots rising high up the calf, favored by riders, marsh travelers, and anyone who wants mud kept at a polite distance. They lend the wearer a certain command, even when the command is simply not ruining their trousers.
flavorText: “Tall boots let a person step through filth with dignity mostly intact.”

name: fancy brogue shoes
shortDescription: Fancy brogue shoes punched and stitched with decorative care, each small pattern declaring that the wearer has opinions about proper entrances. They are charming at court and foolish in bogs, as many charming things are.
flavorText: “Brogues are proof that even shoes can become conversation.”

name: fancy leather boots
shortDescription: Fancy leather boots polished to a warm shine, with cleaner lines than working footwear and more pride than sense on a rainy road. They suit officers, wealthy riders, and merchants who like practical things to know their place below elegance.
flavorText: “Fine leather boots are made for walking just far enough to be admired.”

name: gaiters
shortDescription: Gaiters are protective coverings strapped over lower legs and boots to keep out brush, mud, snow, and other small insults of travel. Scouts and walkers value them because a journey becomes easier when fewer bits of the world get inside your clothes.
flavorText: “Gaiters are modest armor against the road’s petty ambitions.”

name: stockings
shortDescription: Stockings of woven fabric, useful for warmth, modesty, and making formal clothing feel less like punishment. Fine pairs travel wrapped in paper, while common ones vanish mysteriously in washhouses throughout every kingdom.
flavorText: “Stockings prove civilization begins somewhere below the knee.”

name: cotton socks
shortDescription: Cotton socks are light, breathable, and popular among people who walk enough to appreciate small mercies. They lack winter’s heavy comfort but sell steadily wherever boots, sweat, and long workdays meet.
flavorText: “No ballad praises dry socks, which proves ballads are written by fools.”

name: wool socks
shortDescription: Wool socks are thick, warm, and faintly rustic, knitted for cold mornings and stubborn roads. A good pair can make a poor boot feel kinder, which is why winter markets treat them almost like medicine.
flavorText: “Wool socks are hearth-fire for the feet.”

name: chain leggings
shortDescription: Chain leggings hang in flexible mail links, guarding the legs without the full stiffness of plate. They are heavy enough to remind the wearer of danger and useful enough to justify every clinking step.
flavorText: “A sword aimed low meets chain and disappointment.”

name: cloth leggings
shortDescription: Cloth leggings are simple lower garments, easy to dye, easy to mend, and common wherever people value movement over ceremony. They are everyday trade goods, but everyday goods are what keep markets breathing.
flavorText: “Cloth leggings ask no questions and survive many answers.”

name: leather leggings
shortDescription: Leather leggings offer tougher protection than cloth while remaining flexible enough for riders, hunters, and workshop labor. Scuffs improve their honesty, though sellers still pretend polish is the same thing as virtue.
flavorText: “Leather leggings begin stiff and end full of stories.”

name: plate leggings
shortDescription: Plate leggings are shaped metal defenses for thighs and knees, costly to fit and miserable when badly made. Properly forged, they turn a rider’s legs into expensive machinery and make stray blows regret their path.
flavorText: “Plate at the leg is faith in the blacksmith measured by the bruise avoided.”

name: fancy plate leggings
shortDescription: Fancy plate leggings add engraved trim and polished curves to the already difficult business of armored walking. They are made for champions, parade knights, and nobles who want even their knees to participate in reputation.
flavorText: “Plain plate saves the leg; fancy plate insists the leg be remembered.”

name: rough leggings
shortDescription: Rough leggings are patched, sturdy, and meant for people who expect clothing to work before it flatters. They suit charcoal burners, ditch diggers, drovers, and anyone too busy surviving the day to apologize for threadbare knees.
flavorText: “Rough leggings are poor in fabric and rich in excuses.”

name: sandals
shortDescription: Sandals are open, simple footwear for warm roads, bathhouses, gardens, and anyone who trusts the ground more than they should. They pack light and sell well in summer, but they offer little comfort when stones become ambitious.
flavorText: “Sandals are a treaty between the foot and the weather.”

name: mens undertunic
shortDescription: A men’s undertunic is a plain base garment worn beneath better clothing, armor, or whatever dignity a man can afford that day. It is not meant to be displayed, which is why it must be comfortable, washable, and honest.
flavorText: “The undertunic is the garment that does the work while the coat gets praised.”

name: womens chemise undergarment
shortDescription: A women’s chemise undergarment, light and practical, made to sit between skin and the heavier demands of dress. Good cloth matters here more than ornament, because comfort is most valuable where no one applauds it.
flavorText: “A chemise is privacy made washable.”

name: noblewoman blouse
shortDescription: A noblewoman blouse cut from finer cloth, shaped with careful seams and meant to make restraint look expensive. It may appear delicate, but the social arithmetic stitched into such garments is stronger than most buttons.
flavorText: “A noble blouse speaks softly because servants carry the volume.”

name: peasant blouse
shortDescription: A peasant blouse made from plain cloth, loose enough for labor and sturdy enough for repeated washing. Its value is not in rarity but in the way it keeps returning to use after stains, seasons, and small repairs.
flavorText: “A peasant blouse knows the field before it knows the mirror.”

name: cloth tunic
shortDescription: A cloth tunic is ordinary clothing at its most useful: simple, adaptable, and ready for work, travel, or a market day. It can be belted, layered, patched, dyed, or handed down, which makes it quietly indispensable.
flavorText: “The cloth tunic is proof that common things carry most kingdoms.”

name: leather tunic
shortDescription: A leather tunic gives everyday clothing a tougher edge, suited to hunters, drovers, guards, and others who expect brush, sparks, or claws. It wears in slowly and smells faintly of oil, smoke, and practical decisions.
flavorText: “A leather tunic is what cloth becomes after meeting trouble.”

name: monk robes
shortDescription: Monk robes are plain, heavy garments made for long prayer, cold stone halls, and the daily discipline of not caring how one looks. Traders sell them as humble cloth, though buyers know humility comes in surprisingly specific cuts.
flavorText: “Monk robes hide the body so the conscience has fewer excuses.”

name: Frosthaven Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Frosthaven Coat of Arms bears the cold authority of northern walls, winter roads, and families who measure endurance as a virtue. Its colors and marks carry more than decoration; they announce allegiance before a word is spoken.
flavorText: “Frosthaven does not need warmth to recognize its own.”

name: Amberwick Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Amberwick Coat of Arms carries the warm polish of a harbor house grown rich on careful deals and brighter lamps. It is often displayed with civic pride, commercial ambition, and just enough gold thread to make modesty doubtful.
flavorText: “Amberwick’s seal glows best where contracts are being signed.”

name: Gatecrag Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Gatecrag Coat of Arms suggests cliffs, toll gates, and hard stone authority watching every pass. Caravans know the emblem well, usually from banners above roads where coin changes hands before scenery does.
flavorText: “Gatecrag marks the place where the mountain asks its fee.”

name: Appleford Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Appleford Coat of Arms has the easy pride of orchards, cider houses, and green lanes lined with useful trees. It looks gentler than a war banner, but a prosperous harvest can defend a town as surely as spears.
flavorText: “Appleford puts fruit on its shield because hunger is also an enemy.”

name: Emberpeak Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Emberpeak Coat of Arms carries a hot mountain mood, suited to furnaces, red stone, and people who live near fire without calling it a problem. Its emblem feels half civic mark and half warning label.
flavorText: “Emberpeak’s banner does not wave; it smolders.”

name: Pearlgate Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Pearlgate Coat of Arms is elegant, pale, and maritime, recalling tide walls, polished halls, and gates that open more easily for the well-dressed. It sells especially well to captains who want their papers to look respectable at a distance.
flavorText: “Pearlgate wears the sea as jewelry and calls it jurisdiction.”

name: Stormmere Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Stormmere Coat of Arms bears a restless dignity, all deep water, hard weather, and old claims made beside a troubled shore. Its mark is favored by veterans and boatmen who trust calm banners less than storm-tested ones.
flavorText: “Stormmere’s arms remember every gale that failed to sink them.”

name: Painted Coast Unity Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Painted Coast Unity Coat of Arms gathers many coastal loyalties into one careful emblem, handsome enough for ceremony and complicated enough for politics. It is less a simple badge than a negotiated peace stitched into color.
flavorText: “Unity looks cleanest from far away; up close, every thread has an argument.”

name: Glassmere Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Glassmere Coat of Arms has a refined shine, suited to courts, mirrors, and families who know reflection can be more useful than honesty. Its emblem is crisp, delicate, and expensive to reproduce correctly.
flavorText: “Glassmere’s seal flatters the eye while watching it back.”

name: Mirrorfen Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Mirrorfen Coat of Arms carries marshland mystery, still water, and a certain unease about what lies beneath bright surfaces. It is popular with local officials, fen guides, and merchants who prefer their authority slightly eerie.
flavorText: “Mirrorfen banners show twice as much as they explain.”

name: Brasskeep Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Brasskeep Coat of Arms is sturdy, martial, and practical, like a fortified hinge holding the border shut. Its colors suggest metal, walls, discipline, and a town where parade polish is never far from workshop smoke.
flavorText: “Brasskeep knows a shield is only useful if someone maintains the rivets.”

name: Oldwake Harbor Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Oldwake Harbor Coat of Arms feels weathered even when newly made, carrying the memory of docks, salt ropes, and ships that should have retired long ago. It is a proud old mark, more stubborn than fashionable.
flavorText: “Oldwake does not ask if the harbor is ancient; it asks who else remembers.”

name: Riverwake Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Riverwake Coat of Arms has a flowing confidence, fit for ferries, mills, trade barges, and river councils that know water makes its own roads. It is a useful emblem for people whose fortunes move downstream before anyone can tax the current twice.
flavorText: “Riverwake’s banner follows the water and still arrives on time.”

name: Wolfhook Bay Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Wolfhook Bay Coat of Arms is sharp, coastal, and a little predatory, suggesting narrow inlets, hard sailors, and watchfires lit against bad weather. Its emblem suits a place that learned long ago to welcome trade while keeping one hand near a blade.
flavorText: “Wolfhook Bay smiles at ships and counts their teeth.”

name: Embercourt Coat of Arms
shortDescription: The Embercourt Coat of Arms blends courtly polish with the suggestion of banked flame, a heraldic mark for houses that prefer warmth, wealth, and danger in measured amounts. It looks splendid on banners, seals, and any wall that wants to seem recently powerful.
flavorText: “Embercourt keeps its fire indoors and calls the smoke etiquette.”
